source_title: #788: Naval Ravikant and Aaron Stupple — How to Raise a Sovereign Child, A Freedom-Maximizing Approach to Parenting

paragraphs.speaker: tim
paragraphs.name: Tim Ferriss
paragraphs.text: This episode is more of a debate than my usual interviews. I push back on a lot, and that is part of the fun. This is a sharp contrast with the Doctor Becky Kennedy episode on parenting. Many parents want to safely increase the range of freedom for their kids. This is a conversation with someone who is taking a very radical approach to child freedom—one that is based on a real-life parenting movement. Listeners will find some of his recommendations or practices excessive and extreme. However, you'll also likely pick up some ideas for expanding your kids' freedom, creativity, and discovery.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: Aaron Stupple struck me as someone who at first was like, this man is insane—but I realized in a good way. He's a very ground-up, very principled thinker. What I really liked about talking to Aaron is that he will take these philosophically sound positions that are very controversial, and then he will just defend them indefatigably. He is very tied into the idea of using creativity to find answers to problems and to not using coercion. I found myself in the defensive. I found him too radical, too hard to take seriously. But as time went on, I actually realized he was right about a lot of things.

paragraphs.speaker: aaron
paragraphs.name: Aaron Stupple
paragraphs.text: I've got five kids ages seven to one. I started off as a public school teacher coming out of college and spent five years doing that. I was really kind of deep into the human nature and the experience of young people and formed some pretty strong ideas about human nature and children. Along the way, I got into one of our mutual heroes, David Deutsch, and his take on Karl Popper's philosophy. Within that, David Deutsch and his colleague Sarah Fitz-Claridge developed this theory on children and childhood called taking children seriously. My wife and I found ourselves just doing this 100 percent, basically. We've got five kids now. We've been doing this for seven years, and it's remarkable.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: The philosophy that you're talking about—the taking children seriously philosophy, which you lay out in The Sovereign Child—you basically say the kids have no sleep schedules. You don't control what they eat. They have unrestricted screen time. You don't force them to go to school. You don't make them do chores. You don't have rules like don't hit each other. You try not to mediate sibling conflict. You don't force them to share things. They're not obliged to say thank you. There's no real punishment. There's no making them spend time with the grandparents. You don't force them to brush their teeth. You don't make them sit at the dinner table. What are we talking about here? Do you have children or do you have roommates?

paragraphs.speaker: aaron
paragraphs.name: Aaron Stupple
paragraphs.text: The typical way of looking at parenting is the question of what do you allow and what do you disallow. What I do, my wife and I do, is we just step away from that question altogether and instead view problems as they arise and try to find solutions to those problems rather than appealing to rules. The way we interact with our friends and our family—the adults in our lives—we don't apply rules to people. The idea is to let kids choose what is interesting or appealing to them and then deal with problems as they arise.

paragraphs.speaker: aaron
paragraphs.name: Aaron Stupple
paragraphs.text: Every time you force your child to do something, you inevitably set yourself up as an adversary to your kid. If you're trying to get them to eat broccoli, you are introducing a difficulty in their life around food. You want them to learn about broccoli for broccoli's sake. If chocolate makes you feel bad, then you want them to understand that as mediated by themselves, not because you're introducing yourself into that thing. You don't want them to avoid chocolate because they're afraid of dad. If eating well is really important, then it's really important that eating is not confused by what your parents' expectations are.

paragraphs.speaker: aaron
paragraphs.name: Aaron Stupple
paragraphs.text: David Deutsch's perspective is that humans are uniquely knowledge creators. The thing about children that's similar to adults is that they're both knowledge creators in the same way. The role of the parent is to facilitate the child as a burgeoning knowledge creator and not to foil that process. Things that foil that process of knowledge creation and discovery are authorities that arbitrarily thwart you when you're trying to learn about something. The problem with coercion is that it blocks knowledge growth. Your duty as a parent is to facilitate and foster knowledge growth.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: I've incorporated maybe 30 to 50 percent of what you're saying. I basically retreated heavily back.
